statement: vLLM exists because traditional LLM inference pre-allocates contiguous KV-cache memory for the maximum sequence length, wasting 60-80% of GPU memory on internal and external fragmentation. PagedAttention solves this by managing KV cache in fixed-size non-contiguous 'pages' (blocks), similar to OS virtual memory paging. This lets multiple requests share physical memory pages for common prefix tokens and eliminates pre-allocation waste, achieving 24x higher throughput than HuggingFace Transformers at equivalent latency targets.

vLLM is designed as a drop-in OpenAI API replacement: running `python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server --model <model>` starts a server that accepts the same /v1/completions, /v1/chat/completions, and /v1/embeddings endpoints as OpenAI's API. Clients written for OpenAI (LangChain, LlamaIndex, any openai-python SDK user) need only change the base_url and api_key — zero code changes required. This is intentional product strategy: lower adoption friction for teams migrating from OpenAI to self-hosted inference.

vLLM defaults gpu_memory_utilization=0.9, meaning it pre-reserves 90% of GPU VRAM for the KV-cache page pool at startup. Under concurrent high-context requests this headroom is consumed entirely, producing CUDA out-of-memory errors that crash the worker process — not a graceful 429. The fix is NOT to lower gpu_memory_utilization blindly: lowering it reduces throughput. The correct approach is to (1) profile actual peak KV-cache usage with `--max-num-seqs` and `--max-model-len` constraints, (2) set gpu_memory_utilization to leave headroom for CUDA kernels (~5-10%), and (3) configure `--max-num-batched-tokens` to cap total in-flight token count. Many teams hit this on their first multi-user deployment because single-user benchmarks never exhaust the pool.

vLLM treats AWQ, GPTQ, FP8, and GGUF as distinct quantization backends with separate kernel implementations — they are NOT interchangeable. Common failure modes: (1) Loading an AWQ-quantized model without `--quantization awq` causes silent weight corruption or crashes, not a clear error. (2) GPTQ models require `--quantization gptq` and are slower than AWQ on vLLM because vLLM's GPTQ path uses Marlin kernels only on Ampere+, falling back to slow triton on older GPUs. (3) FP8 is only supported on Hopper (H100/H200) and Ada Lovelace (L40S); using `--quantization fp8` on A100 raises an unsupported hardware error. (4) GGUF is not natively supported in the main vLLM server — it requires llama.cpp or conversion first. Always verify the quantization format matches both the `--quantization` flag AND the target GPU architecture before deployment.

vLLM tensor parallelism (--tensor-parallel-size N) requires all N GPUs to have identical VRAM capacity and ideally be connected via NVLink or NVSwitch. Critical gotchas: (1) Mixed GPU types in one tensor-parallel group (e.g., A100-40GB + A100-80GB) will crash because vLLM assumes symmetric sharding — the smaller GPU OOMs during weight load. (2) PCIe-connected GPUs (no NVLink) suffer severe all-reduce latency; for models that fit on a single GPU, single-GPU is faster. (3) `--tensor-parallel-size` must evenly divide the model's attention head count — using TP=3 on a model with 32 heads will raise a configuration error at startup. (4) Pipeline parallelism (--pipeline-parallel-size) is orthogonal to tensor parallelism but multiplies coordination overhead; prefer TP over PP for latency-sensitive workloads.

vLLM uses continuous batching (also called iteration-level scheduling), where new requests are inserted into the active batch the moment a slot frees — no waiting for the entire batch to complete. This is architecturally opposite to static batching (used in naive servers), which holds the GPU idle until every request in a batch finishes its slowest sequence. For mixed-length workloads (typical chat: some 50-token replies, some 2000-token replies), continuous batching delivers 3-10x higher GPU utilization. The implication for operators: latency SLOs should be set per-request (time-to-first-token + inter-token latency), NOT as batch throughput, because individual request latency is now the meaningful unit.

Many models on HuggingFace Hub require `--trust-remote-code` to load custom tokenizers, attention implementations, or architecture files that are not part of vLLM's built-in model registry. Enabling this flag executes arbitrary Python code downloaded from the Hub at model load time — it is a supply-chain attack surface. The security contract is: trust is transitive from the model card to all files in the repo at the pinned revision. Safe practices: (1) Always pin a specific commit SHA (`--revision <sha>`), not a branch or tag, when using trust-remote-code in production. (2) Audit the modeling_*.py files in the repo before deploying. (3) Air-gapped deployments should mirror the model to a private registry and verify checksums. Never use `--trust-remote-code` with `--revision main` in production — main can be updated silently.

vLLM's CPU backend (`--device cpu`) is explicitly experimental and unsuitable for production inference. Key limitations: (1) No PagedAttention on CPU — memory management falls back to eager allocation, so the primary vLLM value proposition disappears. (2) Throughput is 10-50x lower than GPU depending on model size; even a single A10G outperforms a 64-core CPU server for token generation. (3) Quantization support on CPU is limited to INT8 via PyTorch; AWQ and FP8 are GPU-only. (4) Continuous batching scheduler still runs but KV-cache capacity is constrained by system RAM, and swapping between RAM and disk is not implemented in the CPU path. Use the CPU backend only for functional correctness testing or on-device edge deployments where latency requirements are loose (>5s acceptable). For cost-sensitive CPU-only inference, llama.cpp or Ollama are better-architected alternatives.
